title: TPU v1 Public Structure · DFT-Ready Synthesis: Clean Clocks, Resets, and Macro Models before Insertion
oneLine: This case shows how DFT enters its main structural implementation window during synthesis. Stage ⑦ does not replace the complete scan/ATPG flow; it creates a netlist baseline for DFT and backend iteration. Clocks, gating, and resets must be test-controllable, SRAM/IP test models available, and loops, black boxes, and X sources resolved through DFT-ready DRC. TPU v1's actual DFT is unpublished; its public structure only provides context.

legend.name: Test-controllable clocks and gating
legend.desc: Scan shift/capture, MBIST, and at-speed test require controllable clocks. ICGs, PLLs, and derived clocks need explicit test-mode control so no clock island remains inaccessible.

legend.name: Reset and X-source control
legend.desc: Asynchronous resets, uninitialized state, powered-down domains, and black-box outputs may create uncontrollable values or X contamination in test. Define polarity, test behavior, and models before handoff.

legend.name: SRAM / IP test models
legend.desc: Large SRAM is not covered by ordinary logic scan. It needs memory test models, MBIST ports, and optional repair information; third-party IP needs a wrapper or documented black-box test contract.

legend.name: DFT-ready rule checks
legend.desc: Check uncontrollable clocks/resets, combinational loops, latches, tri-states, black boxes, nonscannable registers, and missing scan cells or test models. This is not ordinary lint.

legend.name: Repair and waiver closure
legend.desc: Return real structural issues to RTL, synthesis, constraints, or models. Any unavoidable exception must document impact, verification, owner, and approval; never ignore it silently.

legend.name: Traceable shared DFT/backend baseline
legend.desc: Freeze the synthesized netlist, SDC/UPF, test port/clock/reset definitions, scan/test libraries, macro models, DRC/LEC evidence, and version manifest together so insertion, early ATPG, and backend iteration are reproducible.

steps.cap: ① Define functional, shift, capture, and MBIST clocks and how ICGs/PLLs are controlled safely in test mode.
② Control reset and X sources: define asynchronous controls, powered-down domains, uninitialized state, and black-box outputs so response compression is not contaminated.
③ Complete macro/IP test contracts: SRAM test models, MBIST interfaces, scan cells, and third-party wrappers must match the synthesized netlist version.
④ Run DFT-ready DRC and close every issue through RTL/constraint/model repair or an auditable waiver.
⑤ Freeze a shared baseline—netlist, SDC/UPF, libraries/macros, DRC/LEC, and version manifest—for insertion, early ATPG, and backend coordination.

before: Establish the DFT specification interface: Synthesis must know scan, OCC, MBIST, clock-domain, power-domain, and test-mode requirements before the netlist enters the main insertion and early ATPG window.
Prepare real libraries and models: Target scan cells, ICGs, lockups, memory test models, and third-party IP test information are all required.
Unify netlist and constraint versions: RTL, SDC/UPF, libraries, and black-box models must correspond to one build or DFT DRC cannot be reproduced.
Limit the case honestly: The TPU paper publishes compute array, memory, frequency, and process facts, not specific DFT-ready rules or results.
during: Audit every clock source and gate: List primary and derived clocks, PLLs, ICGs, and asynchronous sources; define controllable paths and bypasses for each test mode.
Audit resets and controls: Ensure asynchronous reset cannot disturb scan shift/capture and test-control signals contain no combinational loop or uncontrollable dependency.
Audit registers and black boxes: Find nonscannable registers, latches, tri-states, combinational feedback, and unknown outputs; repair, wrap, or explicitly exclude them.
Audit memory/IP contracts: Give every SRAM the correct test model and MBIST ports, and every third-party IP an access or bypass mechanism.
Run DFT-ready DRC + LEC: Recheck rules after repair and prove that synthesized output remains functionally equivalent to verified RTL.

result.label: Clocks / resets
result.value: Controllable in functional, shift, capture, and MBIST modes; ICG/PLL bypass defined

result.label: Logic structure
result.value: No unexplained loop, latch, nonscannable register, or blocking black box

result.label: Macros / libraries
result.value: Version-matched scan/test cells, SRAM models, and MBIST/IP interfaces

result.label: Evidence
result.value: No blocking DFT-ready DRC issue + LEC passes + auditable waivers

result.label: Handoff package
result.value: Synthesized netlist + SDC/UPF + test definitions + libraries/models + manifest/tag

after: Enter the main DFT implementation window: Insert scan, compression, test points, OCC, MBIST, and JTAG, then start ATPG; continue iterating with physical design and signoff.
Re-prove every insertion or ECO: After DFT modifies the netlist, rerun functional-mode LEC, post-DFT DRC, and coverage. Never inherit conclusions from the old netlist.
Feed physical constraints back: If congestion, timing, or test power requires chain, compression, or control changes, iterate from the controlled baseline rather than applying ad hoc patches.
Keep final patterns aligned with the final netlist: Regenerate and revalidate ATPG patterns after scan reordering, ECOs, or clock changes.
Preserve honest labeling: Do not present illustrative scan, MBIST, OCC structures, targets, or DRC results as published TPU v1 facts.
source: In-Datacenter Performance Analysis of a TPU, Jouppi et al., ISCA 2017 (published v1 array, memory, frequency, and process data provide structural context only. DFT-ready clock/reset control, macro test models, rule checks, and versioned handoff are standard DFT/synthesis practice, not private TPU v1 disclosure.)
